“calumnies and insults relative to private life.”80 One of the draftsmen of the

78
   A. Franken, Das französische Pfandrecht im Mittelalter (repr. Aalen: Scientia, 1969) (1879), 20-23 and J.
Minier, Précis historique du droit français. Introduction à l’étude du droit (Paris, 1854), 55, 637-638.
79
   French texts treat the protection of privacy, quite erroneously, as a phenomenon dating only to the second
half of the nineteenth century. See e.g, Bernard Beignier, Le Droit de la Personnalité (Paris : Presses
Universitaires de France, 1992), 46-47.
80
   Constitution du 3 Septembre 1791, [ ], 17 : « Les calomnies et injures contre quelques personnes que ce
soit relatives aux actions de leur vie privée, seront punies sur leur poursuite. »

27
 Constitution, the Jacobin Jérôme Pétion, speaking during the flush of revolutionary

excitement in August of 1791, explained the intent of the new document this way. A

vigorous free press was unconditionally necessary for the maintenance of liberal

government. 81 Nevertheless, it was true that those very press liberties threatened the

“private person.”82 For that reason, it was important to confer upon persons whose

private lives had been violated some legal recourse against “insults.” To do so would not

undermine the freedoms gained in the Revolution. On the contrary, it would achieve a

revolutionary end. In fact, the “new doctrine,” with its emphasis on the rights of ordinary

private persons, directly “contradicted the ideas of the ancien régime.” After all:

[In the ancien régime] the least offense given to the honor of a man of position

was a serious crime, which could not be punished severely enough, while an

offense to a simple citizen hardly received any attention on the part of the law.

But for that very reason, the new doctrine is all the more correct and in

conformity to the new order of things. 83

The first introduction of “privacy” into Frenc h law thus came in the form of a classic

statement of the ambition to bring everybody up in status. The introduction of privacy

protections was indeed akin to contemporary developments like the introduction of the

guillotine. The guillotine arrived shortly after Pétion gave this speech, as a means of

extending the high-status privilege of beheading to all persons. 84 It too belonged to a

“new order of things” in which everybody’s honor was to be protected.

81
   J. Pétion, Discours sur la liberté de la Presse, in Courier de Provence, vol. 16, no. 325, 169-192.
82
   Id., no. 326, 199.
83
   Id., 199.
84
   Whitman, Harsh Justice.

28
          These remained the characteristic ideas of French privacy law thereafter—notably

in 1819, the year of the passage of the first post-Napoleonic law lifting press censorship.

As the Restoration successfully established its authority, the government of Louis

Philippe consented to tolerate a freer press. But the idea of press freedom continued to
